title: Routing a groove for a plywood panel insert
subtitle: Run a router with a straight bit along a fence guide to cut a housing groove in timber for a floating plywood or hardboard panel
excerpt: How to use a router with a fence guide to cut a straight housing groove for a plywood panel insert. Covers setting the bit depth and fence distance, making test cuts, and routing the full groove in one or two passes.
sourceNotes: Routing technique from Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1905, public domain). UK joinery trade practice for panel-and-frame construction. The Workshop Companion router guide (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A rectangular-section channel cut along the length of a timber member to receive and locate a panel, shelf, or divider. In frame-and-panel construction, the housing groove runs the full length of the stile or rail and holds the panel edges; the panel floats in the groove without being glued, allowing it to expand and contract with humidity without splitting the frame. The groove is typically one-third of the timber thickness deep and matched in width to the panel thickness (6 mm for 6 mm plywood, 9 mm for 9 mm MDF.

content.text: locates a panel in a timber frame without glue, so the panel can move with humidity. The approach is setting up the router fence guide to run parallel to the timber edge, making a test cut in scrap to confirm the depth and position, and routing the groove in one or two passes.

Fit a straight-flute router bit matched to the panel thickness) a 6 mm bit for 6 mm ply, 9 mm for 9 mm. Set the bit depth to one-third of the timber thickness: a 45 mm stile takes a 15 mm deep groove. Set the fence guide distance from the bit to the desired groove position (typically 8 to 10 mm from the inner face of the frame member. Clamp a piece of scrap and make a test cut to check depth and position before routing the actual workpiece.

Clamp the stile or rail flat on the bench with G-cramps) one at each end (so the fence face has a clear run from end to end. The fence guide bears against the edge of the timber as the router travels; any wobble in the fence line produces a wavy groove. Mark the start and stop points with tape if the groove does not run full length.

Feed the router against the bit rotation) right to left when the fence is on the near edge. Keep the fence pressed firmly against the timber edge throughout the cut. In hardwood or at the full groove depth, take two passes: the first at half depth, the second to full depth. In softwood or MDF, one pass at the full 15 mm is usually clean enough.

Slide the panel edge into the groove: it should fit with finger pressure but not need forcing. A panel that is loose will rattle; a panel that needs mallet work will prevent assembly when both stiles are glued up. If the groove is slightly tight, pare each wall of the groove with a sharp chisel to widen by 0.5 mm increments until the fit is right.
